title: Key Vault soft-delete disabled + privileged RBAC
narrative: Key Vault has soft-delete disabled. A privileged principal (or one compromised via any chain) deletes the vault. Every secret, cert, and key inside is gone permanently — the classic ransomware 'destroy the evidence' move, except for all cryptographic material.

steps.action: Call DELETE /vaults/<name>.
Every app dependent on vault secrets fails.
steps.gain: Extended outage + forced key rotation for whatever was stored.

blast_radius.max_privilege: Destructive — not escalation, but irrecoverable loss.
data_at_risk: Every secret, cert, key in vault
services_at_risk: Any app that pulls from the vault

title: Storage account SAS token with long TTL + URL leak
narrative: Storage generates SAS tokens with expiry > 90 days AND blob public access is off (private by design). A SAS token leaked into a log, browser history, or URL referrer still unlocks the storage account for three months, bypassing firewall rules.

steps.action: Find the SAS URL in a leaked HAR / web archive / log file.
Use the SAS to download every blob, bypassing private endpoint or firewall.

blast_radius.initial_access: SAS URL in any leaked artifact.
blast_radius.max_privilege: Whatever the SAS was scoped to.

title: Cosmos DB account key exposed in App Service config
narrative: Cosmos DB is accessed via master account key stored in App Service application settings. The key is readable by anyone with Website Contributor and grants root access to the entire Cosmos account. Managed identity would sandbox this; the developer chose the shortcut.

steps.action: Read app settings; find CosmosDBKey value.
Use key via Cosmos SDK; full read + delete on every container.

data_at_risk: Every Cosmos container in the account

title: SQL Server firewall 0.0.0.0-255.255.255.255
narrative: An Azure SQL Server has a firewall rule opening 0.0.0.0 to 255.255.255.255 and uses SQL authentication (no Entra integration). Any attacker with credentials — or success against a password-spray — has a direct connection string from anywhere on the internet.

steps.action: Connect to <server>.database.windows.net:1433 from anywhere.
Spray credentials against SQL auth.
Read, alter, or xp_cmdshell-style escalation depending on role.

title: Data Factory linked service with cleartext credential
narrative: An ADF pipeline uses a linked service that stores DB credentials inline instead of referencing Key Vault. Anyone with Data Factory Reader on the workspace can read the linked service JSON and extract the password. No vault rotation, no CA gate, no alert.

steps.action: Export pipeline + linked service JSON via portal or API.
Connect directly to the referenced database.
steps.gain: No trace — DF audit log doesn't cover external DB sessions.

blast_radius.max_privilege: DB role granted to the credential.
services_at_risk: Any data store linked to this ADF

title: Databricks workspace token sprawl + public access
narrative: Databricks workspace uses public network access for the control plane and users generate long-lived personal access tokens. A leaked token lets an attacker run arbitrary Spark jobs, which have cluster-level credentials to read any data source the workspace connects to.

steps.action: Harvest a PAT from a developer laptop or CI pipeline log.
Submit a Spark job that dbutils.fs reads every mounted storage.

blast_radius.max_privilege: Whatever the cluster IAM role grants.

title: Synapse SQL pool without TDE + public endpoint
narrative: A Synapse dedicated SQL pool is reachable over the public endpoint AND Transparent Data Encryption is disabled. A compromised DBA session yields unencrypted BAK files, and any backup exfil to attacker storage is plaintext.

steps.action: BACKUP DATABASE to URL pointing at attacker storage.
RESTORE locally; no TDE decryption needed.

title: Stream Analytics input with embedded storage key
narrative: A Stream Analytics job reads from a storage account using an embedded account key rather than managed identity. A job export leaks the key; the storage account has a loose SAS policy. One exposure chains into long-term account access.

steps.action: Export ASA job definition; extract account key.
Use key to mint fresh SAS; access any container.

title: Redis cache exposed without AUTH
narrative: An Azure Cache for Redis has public network access enabled, and the developer enabled non-SSL port 6379 or disabled AUTH. Redis is internet-reachable, no auth required. Any attacker can KEYS *, read session tokens, and impersonate users.

steps.action: Shodan-scan *.redis.cache.windows.net for 6379.
KEYS * and GET session:*; harvest tokens.
steps.gain: Session tokens for any app backed by this Redis.

services_at_risk: Apps using this Redis for sessions/cache

title: HDInsight cluster without VNet injection
narrative: HDInsight cluster nodes are reachable from the public internet and authenticate with cluster-local SSH keys. A weak SSH config yields a Hadoop worker shell which has credentials to every storage account the cluster writes to.

steps.action: SSH to *-ssh.azurehdinsight.net; spray weak passwords.
Use Hadoop CLI to read/write connected storage accounts.

title: Purview account with public access + privileged collections
narrative: Azure Purview catalogs data sources — reading the catalog itself leaks the table names, schemas, and classification labels of sensitive data. If Purview is publicly reachable AND audit logs aren't on, an attacker can reconnoitre the entire data estate.

steps.action: Probe Purview REST API with any token.
Target the highest-classification assets listed; pivot to chain exploits.

title: MariaDB server with public endpoint + SSL off
narrative: A MariaDB flexible server is reachable over the public internet and client SSL enforcement is disabled. An attacker who obtains the connection string gets clear-text credentials over the wire, and on-path adversaries can sniff traffic too.

steps.action: Locate the connection string in an app repo.
Connect over plaintext; read any table.
steps.gain: Database content + sniffable session for follow-on creds.

title: Data Lake Gen2 with container-level public access
narrative: A Data Lake Storage Gen2 account has one container set to Blob public access. Gen2's hierarchical namespace means the entire directory tree under that container is readable anonymously. Enterprises often discover this AFTER the data has been indexed by search engines.

steps.action: Discover the container name via enumeration or search indexing.
Walk the hierarchical namespace; download entire folder trees.

title: NetApp volume NFSv3 without export policy scoping
narrative: A NetApp volume uses NFSv3 (IP-based auth only) and its export policy allows any subnet in the VNet. A compromised VM in any peered VNet can mount the volume without per-user auth.

steps.action: mount -o nfsvers=3 <netapp>:/vol /mnt.
Read every file, no audit trail of per-user access.

title: Managed disk snapshot with overly-permissive SAS
narrative: A managed disk snapshot is exported via SAS URL with Read access + 7-day TTL. The URL ends up in a support ticket. Anyone with the URL downloads the full disk image — including OS, app secrets, and any cached database files.

steps.action: Find the SAS URL in a leaked email / ticket.
Download and mount the VHD locally.
steps.gain: Full disk offline analysis — extract cached passwords, keys, data.

services_at_risk: Any app whose state was on the disk

title: Cross-region replication to unauthorised region
narrative: A storage account is configured for GRS to a region outside the tenant's data residency commitments. A compliance auditor flags this as a data-sovereignty breach — but operationally the data is also reachable from the secondary region, doubling the attack surface.

steps.action: GRS writes continuous copies to a non-approved region.
Compromise the secondary endpoint; same content as primary.

title: Table Storage with soft-delete off + public access
narrative: Table Storage has no soft-delete protection AND public read is enabled. A ransomware actor who reaches any RBAC writer can simply delete all rows — no undelete, no restore, full data loss.

steps.action: Authenticate with a leaked SAS or compromised RBAC.
DELETE every partition key batch; no recovery available.

title: Blob versioning off + immutability off + writable
narrative: A storage container has blob versioning and immutability both off AND a compromised key. A ransomware operator overwrites every blob with encrypted content. Original data gone — this is 'ransomware by API call'.

steps.action: Authenticate via SAS/account key.
PUT encrypted content over every existing blob; previous version unreachable.

title: Column-level encryption miss on PII table
narrative: A SQL table holds PII in a column without Always Encrypted. A DBA role can read the plaintext. Database role compromise turns a 'authorised DBA session' into mass PII exfil with no crypto boundary to slow the attacker.

steps.action: SELECT * FROM sensitive_table.
Bulk export to attacker storage; no Always Encrypted to block.

services_at_risk: Any app keeping regulated PII in SQL

title: Cosmos DB multi-region replication to wrong region
narrative: Cosmos DB has a secondary replica in a region outside data-residency commitments. The replica is still ARM-addressable; a compromised principal targets the off-region endpoint which may have weaker VNet rules.

steps.action: Enumerate Cosmos accounts; find the off-region writeable replica.
Authenticate + read; same data as primary.
steps.gain: Read-path circumvention of the primary's hardening.

title: Legacy File Share SMB exposed outside trusted network
narrative: An Azure Files share uses SMB and is reachable over public IP addresses (firewall allows Azure services + public). Any compromised VM can mount the share with a captured account key.

steps.action: net use \\account.file.core.windows.net\share /user:AZURE\account <key>.
Walk share, exfiltrate, or deploy ransomware.

title: Immutability lock too short for retention requirements
narrative: Blob immutability policy is set but retention is shorter than required retention (e.g. 30 days when SOX requires 7 years). Auditors flag this as a compliance gap; operationally, the window during which data can be deleted is earlier than expected.

steps.action: Immutability window expires; blobs become deletable.
Delete or overwrite; pre-retention-period exposure.
steps.gain: Data destruction before audit retention met.

title: TDE with customer-managed key + Key Vault purge protection off
narrative: A SQL server uses TDE with a customer-managed key stored in a Key Vault where purge protection is off. An attacker-admin purges the vault, permanently losing the key. TDE data is then unreadable forever — cryptographic self-destruct.

steps.action: Delete the Key Vault.
Purge the soft-deleted vault.
steps.gain: Permanent key loss; all TDE-encrypted data unreadable.

data_at_risk: All TDE-encrypted DBs using this key

title: Queue Storage without encryption-at-rest enforcement
narrative: Queue Storage handles transient messages that often carry secrets (webhook payloads, user tokens). Encryption-at-rest is platform-default but key rotation is not enforced. A compromised Azure-layer subpoena + key theft yields historical queue content.

steps.action: Compromise key rotation process or obtain KV access.
Decrypt historical queue snapshots.

title: Lake House table with no row-level security on multi-tenant data
narrative: A Synapse or Databricks table holds multi-tenant data with a tenant_id column but no row-level security filter. Any query user reads every tenant's rows — a classic tenant-isolation bug that audits rarely catch because 'the data is in Azure'.

steps.action: SELECT * FROM shared_table WHERE 1=1.
Bulk export; tenant separation violated.
